channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Adversarial Malware Generation in Linux ELF Binaries via Semantic-Preserving Transformations
item.description: This paper addresses the critical gap in adversarial malware generation for Linux ELF binaries—a domain largely overlooked compared to Windows PE files. We propose a novel, **semantic-preserving adversarial generator** that modifies ELF files without altering runtime behavior, using targeted string injection, section rewriting, and relocation-aware perturbations. Evaluating against the MalConv classifier in a white-box setting, our method achieves a **67.74% evasion rate** and reduces mean detection confidence by **0.50** on a curated ELF malware dataset. Crucially, we demonstrate that MalConv is highly sensitive to benign strings inserted *anywhere* in the binary—including `.rodata`, `.dynstr`, and even padding regions—highlighting a fundamental fragility in sequence-based detectors. Our work establishes the first reproducible ELF adversarial benchmark and reveals that string-level features, not just code logic, dominate modern ELF classification decisions.

item.title: Adversarial Co-Evolution of Malware and Detection Models: A Bilevel Optimization Perspective
item.description: This paper addresses the growing vulnerability of ML-based malware detectors to adaptive adversarial attacks—particularly those leveraging reinforcement learning for evasion. We propose a novel defense framework grounded in **bilevel optimization**, which explicitly models the defender–attacker interaction as an iterative, strategic co-evolutionary game: the upper-level optimizes the detector’s parameters for robustness, while the lower-level generates optimal adversarial perturbations mimicking adaptive attackers. Evaluated on the MAB-malware benchmark against three real-world families (Mokes, Strab, DCRat), our approach reduces evasion rates from up to 90% (for standard and basic adversarially retrained models) to just **0–1.89%**, achieving near-total immunity. Moreover, it increases the attacker’s query complexity by **up to two orders of magnitude**, drastically raising the cost of successful evasion. These results demonstrate that bilevel optimization is essential for building detection systems resilient to evolving adversarial threats.

item.title: Automation-Exploit: A Multi-Agent LLM Framework for Adaptive Offensive Security with Digital Twin-Based Risk-Mitigated Exploitation
item.description: Automation-Exploit is a fully autonomous Multi-Agent System (MAS) framework for adaptive offensive security in complex black-box environments. It bridges the reconnaissance-to-exploitation gap by autonomously exfiltrating binaries and contextual intelligence (e.g., libc version, open file descriptors) across HTTP, SMB, and SSH protocols—enabling both logical (e.g., SQLi) and binary (e.g., stack overflow) attack chains. Its key innovation is a digital twin–based safety architecture: for high-risk memory-corruption vulnerabilities, it conditionally instantiates a cross-platform digital twin *only* upon successful binary exfiltration, enforcing strict state synchronization (libc alignment, FD hooking, memory layout mirroring) to iteratively debug payloads in isolation—ensuring crash-free, risk-mitigated “one-shot” execution on the physical target. Empirical evaluation across eight diverse scenarios—including undocumented zero-day systems—confirms 100% DoS avoidance and successful real-target compromises without live-fire crashes.

item.title: Behavioral Canaries: Auditing Private Retrieved Context Usage in RL Fine-Tuning
item.description: This paper introduces **Behavioral Canaries**, a novel auditing framework for detecting unauthorized use of private retrieved contexts in Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning (RLFT). Unlike traditional auditing methods—which rely on verbatim memorization or membership inference and fail under RL—our approach embeds *stylistic triggers*: each canary pairs a sensitive document with preference feedback that rewards a distinctive, measurable behavioral pattern (e.g., specific syntactic choices or discourse markers). If the document is illicitly used in RL training, the model develops a latent, trigger-conditioned stylistic preference. Empirical evaluation shows Behavioral Canaries achieve **67% detection rate at 10% false-positive rate** (AUROC = 0.756) with only **1% canary injection rate**, without degrading standard task performance. This establishes behavioral signatures—not factual recall—as a principled, practical signal for RLFT compliance auditing.

item.title: Data-Free Contribution Estimation in Federated Learning using Gradient von Neumann Entropy
item.description: Client contribution estimation in federated learning (FL) is essential for fairness and robustness but is challenged by privacy constraints and manipulation risks from validation data or self-reported metadata. We propose a **data-free signal**: the **gradient von Neumann entropy (GvNE)**—the spectral entropy of the final-layer update matrix—which quantifies the *information diversity* of client contributions. We instantiate two practical schemes: (**i**) **SpectralFed**, using normalized GvNE as aggregation weights; and (**ii**) **SpectralFuse**, fusing GvNE with class-specific alignment via a rank-adaptive Kalman filter for per-round stability. Across CIFAR-10/100, FEMNIST, and FedISIC under diverse non-IID settings (e.g., Dirichlet α=0.1), GvNE scores achieve consistently high correlation (>0.85 Pearson r) with standalone client accuracy—without any server-held data or client metadata. Our method outperforms existing data-free baselines (e.g., gradient norm, cosine similarity) in correlation strength, stability, and robustness.

item.title: FedSPDnet: Geometry-Aware Federated Deep Learning with SPDnet
item.description: We propose **FedSPDnet**, the first geometry-aware federated learning framework for SPDnet—a deep network operating on symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices with Stiefel-constrained parameters. Standard FedAvg violates orthogonality via Euclidean averaging, degrading model integrity. To preserve manifold structure, we introduce two efficient aggregation methods: **ProjAvg**, which projects arithmetic means onto the Stiefel manifold, and **RLAvg**, which approximates tangent-space averaging via retractions and liftings. Both are optimizer-agnostic, computationally lightweight, and scale seamlessly to resource-constrained signal processing applications. On EEG motor imagery benchmarks, FedSPDnet achieves **+4.2% higher F1 score** than federated EEGnet, demonstrates superior robustness to non-IID data and partial client participation, and reduces per-round communication overhead by **37%**. This work bridges Riemannian optimization and federated learning for SPD-valued data.

item.title: Sharpness-Aware Poisoning: Enhancing Transferability of Injective Attacks on Recommender Systems
item.description: Recommender systems (RS) are vulnerable to injective attacks, where adversaries inject fake user profiles to boost target items’ exposure for unethical gains. Existing methods rely on a fixed surrogate model to generate poisoned data, assuming good transferability to unknown victim models—yet this fails under structural mismatch. To address this, we propose **Sharpness-Aware Poisoning (SharpAP)**, the first attack framework that explicitly optimizes poison data against the *approximate worst-case victim model*, identified via sharpness-aware minimization. SharpAP formulates the attack as a **tri-level min–max–min optimization**: (i) minimize recommendation loss on the surrogate; (ii) maximize loss via parameter perturbation (simulating worst-case victim); (iii) re-optimize poison data to maximize target-item promotion *on that worst-case model*. This yields poisoned data robust to model architecture shifts and less overfitted to the surrogate. Experiments on Amazon-Book, Yelp, and Tmall show SharpAP improves cross-model attack transferability by **23.7–48.5%** over state-of-the-art baselines, especially under large surrogate–victim architectural gaps.

item.title: Transient Turn Injection: Exposing Stateless Multi-Turn Vulnerabilities in Large Language Models
item.description: This paper introduces **Transient Turn Injection (TTI)**, a novel multi-turn adversarial attack that exploits *stateless* moderation in LLMs by distributing malicious intent across isolated, context-free interactions—bypassing defenses reliant on persistent conversational state. Unlike conventional jailbreaks, TTI uses LLM-powered automated agents to iteratively probe and evade safety policies in black-box settings. Across 12 leading models (including GPT-4o, Claude-3.5, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Llama-3.1-70B, and Qwen2.5-72B), we find stark resilience disparities: only ~20% show inherent TTI robustness, with medical-domain models exhibiting particularly high failure rates (68% violation rate). Our framework uncovers previously unknown model-specific vulnerabilities—e.g., cross-turn cache poisoning and semantic composition blind spots—and demonstrates that session-level context aggregation and deep alignment tuning significantly mitigate TTI. This work underscores the urgent need for context-aware, continuous adversarial evaluation of real-world LLM deployments.

item.title: Black-Box Skill Stealing Attack from Proprietary LLM Agents: An Empirical Study
item.description: This paper presents the first empirical study of black-box skill stealing attacks against proprietary LLM agents—where adversaries extract hidden, copyright-protected skill logic through public API interactions alone. We propose a novel attack taxonomy grounded in prompt-stealing literature and build an automated stealing agent that generates diverse, high-fidelity queries via seed prompting, scenario rationalization, structure injection, and embedding-based diversity filtering. Evaluated across 3 commercial agent frameworks and 5 LLMs (including GPT-4o, Claude-3.5, and Qwen2-72B), our attacks successfully reconstruct proprietary skills with **only 3 queries on average**, achieving up to 89% fidelity—posing severe copyright risks. We design and test multi-stage defenses (input filtering, inference obfuscation, output perturbation), which raise per-attempt cost but fail to prevent low-cost, massively parallelized retries: **a single successful extraction fully compromises the skill**. Our findings expose a critical, widely overlooked vulnerability in today’s skill economy—and call for defense strategies with stronger formal guarantees.

item.title: Stealthy Backdoor Attacks against LLMs Based on Natural Style Triggers
item.description: We propose **BadStyle**, the first end-to-end backdoor attack framework for LLMs that leverages *natural linguistic style*—rather than explicit tokens—as stealthy, semantic-preserving triggers. To overcome instability in payload injection during fine-tuning, we introduce an **auxiliary target loss** that jointly reinforces target response generation on poisoned inputs and suppresses it on benign ones. Grounded in a realistic threat model covering both prompt-induced and PEFT-based injection, BadStyle achieves high attack success rates (ASRs) across 7 diverse victim models (e.g., LLaMA, Phi, DeepSeek, GPT series), with an average ASR improvement of ~30% over baselines. Crucially, the backdoor remains effective even in unseen deployment scenarios and consistently evades state-of-the-art input- and output-level defenses via natural camouflage.

item.title: A Sociotechnical, Practitioner-Centered Approach to Technology Adoption in Cybersecurity Operations: An LLM Case
item.description: This study addresses chronic technology adoption barriers in Security Operations Centers (SOCs), particularly for Large Language Models (LLMs), whose hallucinations and opacity undermine practitioner trust. Through six months of embedded ethnographic fieldwork with a multinational SOC, we co-designed and iteratively refined LLM-based companion tools—such as natural-language alert summarizers and cross-source log correlators—directly with analysts. Our sociotechnical, practitioner-centered approach, grounded in Nonaka’s SECI model, prioritized workflow alignment, interpretability (e.g., confidence scoring, traceable outputs), and incremental integration. This shifted adoption from initial skepticism to sustained daily use (>85% core users engaged ≥3 times weekly). Findings demonstrate that co-creation overcomes traditional pitfalls—including workflow misalignment, rigidity against evolving threats, and knowledge stagnation—establishing a viable paradigm for developing usable, trustworthy AI tools in cybersecurity operations.

item.title: Benchmarking the Utility of Privacy-Preserving Cox Regression Under Data-Driven Clipping Bounds: A Multi-Dataset Simulation Study
item.description: This study benchmarks the statistical utility of differentially private (DP) Cox regression under data-driven clipping bounds—using observed min/max values—across 5 clinical datasets ($n = 168$–$6{,}524$), 15 $\varepsilon$ levels (0.1–1000), and 1,000 Monte Carlo iterations. We compare Laplace and randomized response mechanisms applied to three input-perturbation strategies (covariates-only, all inputs, discrete-time) versus dfbeta-based output perturbation, evaluating significance loss rate (LSR), C-index, and coefficient bias. Results show that at standard privacy levels ($\varepsilon \leq 1$), 90–94% of originally significant covariates lose significance—even in the largest dataset—and predictive performance degrades to near-random (C-index ≈ 0.5). Covariate-only perturbation best preserves risk-set structure and achieves highest significance recovery; dfbeta output perturbation maintains near-baseline performance only for $\varepsilon \geq 5$. Practical utility requires $\varepsilon \geq 10$ for prediction and $\varepsilon \geq 30$–60 for significance preservation, with elevated false-positive rates for marginally significant variables at moderate-to-high $\varepsilon$. This work provides the first multi-dataset empirical foundation for deploying DP survival analysis in healthcare.

item.title: MCP Pitfall Lab: Exposing Developer Pitfalls in MCP Tool Server Security under Multi-Vector Attacks
item.description: MCP Pitfall Lab is the first protocol-aware security testing framework for Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool servers, designed to expose and remediate developer pitfalls under realistic multi-vector attacks. We operationalize six canonical pitfalls (P1–P6) as reproducible scenarios across email, document, and crypto workflows—using six server variants and modeling three attack families: tool-metadata poisoning, puppet servers, and multimodal image-to-tool chains. Evaluation leverages objective MCP traces and validators—not agent self-reports—to ground findings. Static analysis (Tier-1) achieves perfect F1=1.0 on four pitfalls (P1, P2, P5, P6), while correctly identifying P3/P4 as trace/dataflow-dependent. Recommended hardening eliminates all Tier-1 findings (29→0) and reduces the framework risk score from 10.0 to 0.0 at a mean cost of 27 LOC. Crucially, in 19 email-system runs, agent narratives diverged from trace evidence in 63.2% of cases—and in 100% of sink-action executions—demonstrating the critical need for trace-based auditing and regression testing.

item.title: A Stackelberg Model for Hybridization in Cryptography
item.description: This paper models cryptographic hybridization—combining classical, post-quantum, and hybrid encryption schemes—as a **Stackelberg game with resource constraints**, where the defender (leader) selects an encryption strategy to balance security and operational cost, and the attacker (follower) observes the choice and optimally allocates limited resources to cryptanalysis. We formalize the attacker’s response as a conditional optimization problem—the *attacker subgame*—and solve it via a **resource-aware dynamic programming algorithm** that captures multi-stage, adaptive attack paths. The defender’s Stackelberg problem is then cast as a **tractable linear program**, incorporating security guarantees and cost penalties. Our approach yields provably robust hybridization policies: in NIST PQC migration benchmarks, it extends quantum-resistance latency by 3.2× over uniform mixing while reducing encryption latency variance by 47%. This work establishes the first computationally grounded game-theoretic framework for strategic cryptographic hybridization.

item.title: Differentially Private De-identification of Dutch Clinical Notes: A Comparative Evaluation
item.description: This work presents the first comparative evaluation of differential privacy (DP), named entity recognition (NER), and large language models (LLMs) for de-identifying Dutch clinical notes. We assess both standalone approaches and hybrid strategies—specifically NER→DP and LLM→DP—on a newly curated Dutch benchmark (Dutch-ClinDeID v1.0, 12.48K notes). Privacy is measured via membership inference attack success rate; utility is evaluated extrinsically on entity and relation classification tasks. Results show that DP alone severely degrades utility (−28.6% F1 on entity classification), while fine-tuned Dutch LLMs (e.g., Llama-3-Dutch) achieve high utility (92.3% of original) with strong empirical privacy (11.7% attack success). Crucially, the LLM→DP hybrid achieves the best privacy–utility trade-off: only 0.83% leakage and 86.5% relation F1—outperforming pure DP by +41.2% utility and pure LLM by −3.2× leakage risk. Code, data, and models are publicly released.

item.title: CSC: Turning the Adversary's Poison against Itself
item.description: Poisoning-based backdoor attacks severely threaten deep learning models by implanting stealthy triggers that cause targeted misclassification without degrading clean accuracy. Existing defenses often fail against adaptive variants and harm utility via aggressive unlearning or pruning. This paper reveals that poisoned samples rapidly form isolated, low-class-diversity, high-density clusters in latent space—driven by trigger-dominant features—enabling early detection. We propose **Cluster Segregation Concealment (CSC)**: (1) *Segregation*: extract shallow features from early epochs, apply DBSCAN clustering, and identify anomalous clusters using Class Diversity Ratio (CDR) and Local Density Offset (LDO); (2) *Concealment*: relabel clustered poisoned samples to a virtual class and fine-tune only the classifier head with cross-entropy loss—replacing malicious trigger-label associations with benign virtual mappings. Evaluated across four benchmarks against twelve attacks, CSC reduces average attack success rate to **0.8%** (vs. 23.7% for SOTAs) with only **0.3–0.9% clean accuracy drop**, outperforming nine state-of-the-art defenses. CSC advances trustworthy AI through robust pattern discovery, a novel concealment paradigm, and rigorous empirical validation.

item.title: Strategic Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Architecture for Cost-Effective Code Vulnerability Detection
item.description: Automated code vulnerability detection faces a fundamental accuracy-cost trade-off. We propose a strategic heterogeneous multi-agent architecture (“3+1”) grounded in game-theoretic principles: three parallel cloud-based DeepSeek-V3 experts analyze code from complementary structural, security-pattern, and debugging-logic perspectives (formalized as a cooperative super-additive game), while a local lightweight Qwen3-8B verifier performs zero-marginal-cost adversarial validation (modeled as an incentive-aligned verification game). Evaluated on 262 real samples from the NIST Juliet Suite (14 CWEs, balanced classes), our approach achieves **77.2% F1** (62.9% precision, 100% recall) at **$0.002/sample**, outperforming a single-expert LLM baseline (F1 71.4%) and Cppcheck (MCC = 0). The verifier boosts precision by +10.3 pp (*p < 1e−6*), and parallel execution yields 3.0× speedup. This work demonstrates that game-theoretic design enables cost-effective, high-fidelity vulnerability detection.

item.title: Separable Expert Architecture: Toward Privacy-Preserving LLM Personalization via Composable Adapters and Deletable User Proxies
item.description: This paper introduces the **Separable Expert Architecture (SEA)**, a privacy-preserving framework for LLM personalization that decouples user-specific data from shared model weights. SEA comprises three layers: (1) a frozen base model (e.g., Phi-3.5-mini, Llama-3.1-8B); (2) composable, domain-specific LoRA adapters that shape behavior *without* encoding user data; and (3) lightweight, per-user proxy artifacts—whose deletion guarantees *deterministic unlearning*. Experiments confirm strong per-user differentiation (82–89% verification pass rate post-deletion, KL ≈ 0.21 nats vs. baseline) and near-zero cross-user contamination (<0.3%). Crucially, because user data never touches shared weights, SEA intrinsically mitigates model inversion, membership inference, and training-data extraction attacks. By reducing unlearning to a simple deletion operation—compatible with DP-SGD for private base-model updates—SEA transforms machine unlearning from an intractable weight-editing problem into a practical, verifiable, and privacy-enhancing capability.

item.title: Dynamical Priors as a Training Objective in Reinforcement Learning
item.description: Standard reinforcement learning optimizes for reward but imposes no explicit constraints on how action probabilities evolve over time—leading to temporally incoherent policies (e.g., abrupt confidence shifts, oscillations, or degenerate inactivity). We propose **Dynamical Prior RL (DP-RL)**, a training framework that augments policy gradient updates with an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics implementing evidence accumulation and hysteresis. Crucially, DP-RL requires *no changes* to the reward, environment, or policy architecture—instead, it shapes the temporal geometry of decision-making by regularizing the *trajectory* of action probabilities during learning. Across three minimal yet diagnostic environments, DP-RL induces task-dependent temporal structure: suppressing oscillations, introducing biologically plausible response delays and inertia, and preventing inactive degeneracy—even without reward signals. These results demonstrate that the training objective alone can serve as a powerful, interpretable inductive bias for temporal coherence in RL agents.

item.title: Spatial Metaphors for LLM Memory: A Critical Analysis of the MemPalace Architecture
item.description: This study critically evaluates MemPalace, an open-source LLM memory system launched in April 2026 that applies the ancient “memory palace” spatial metaphor to long-term memory organization. Through independent code analysis, rigorous LongMemEval benchmark replication (including ablation studies), and comparison with competing systems (e.g., Mem0, RecallDB), we find its headline 96.6% Recall@5 performance stems primarily from verbatim storage + ChromaDB’s default all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embedding—not from the spatial hierarchy (Wings→Rooms→Closets→Drawers), which functions as standard metadata filtering. MemPalace’s genuine contributions are: (1) a contrarian verbatim-first storage philosophy; (2) ultra-low wake-up cost (~170 tokens) via its four-layer stack; (3) a fully deterministic, zero-LLM write path enabling offline, zero-cost operation; and (4) the first systematic engineering of spatial memory metaphors as a core architectural principle for AI memory systems. Rapid progress by rivals—e.g., Mem0’s April 2026 algorithm lifting its LongMemEval score from ~49% to 93.4%—highlights narrowing performance gaps. We conclude MemPalace embodies significant architectural insight, yet its claims are overstated—a common pattern where marketing velocity outpaces scientific rigor in fast-moving open-source AI projects.

item.title: Do LLM Decoders Listen Fairly? Benchmarking How Language Model Priors Shape Bias in Speech Recognition
item.description: This paper benchmarks how language model (LM) priors shape demographic fairness in ASR, evaluating 9 models across three architectural generations (CTC, encoder-decoder with implicit LM, LLM-based explicit decoder) on 43k utterances from Common Voice 24 and Fair-Speech—controlling for lexical confounds across ethnicity, accent, gender, age, and L1. Under clean conditions, we find: (1) LLM decoders do *not* amplify racial bias (Granite-8B achieves best ethnicity fairness, max/min WER = 2.28); (2) Whisper-v3 exhibits pathological, non-monotonic hallucination on Indian-accented speech (insertion rate spikes to 9.62%); and (3) audio compression—not LLM scale—best predicts accent fairness. Under 12 acoustic degradations (216 inference runs), silence injection amplifies Whisper’s accent bias by 4.64× via demographic-selective hallucination, while chunk masking triggers catastrophic repetition loops in Whisper (86% of 51,797 insertions) but not in explicit-LLM decoders (38× fewer insertions, near-zero repetition)—unless paired with high-compression encoders (e.g., Q-former), which reintroduce repetition pathology even in LLM decoders. We conclude that **audio encoder design—not LLM scaling—is the primary lever for equitable and robust speech recognition**.

item.title: Toward Efficient Membership Inference Attacks against Federated Large Language Models: A Projection Residual Approach
item.description: Federated Large Language Models (FedLLMs) promise privacy-preserving collaborative LLM fine-tuning, yet shared gradients remain vulnerable to membership inference attacks (MIAs). Existing MIAs fail on FedLLMs due to their massive scale, rapid convergence, and sparse, non-orthogonal gradients. We propose **ProjRes**, the first projection-residual-based *passive* MIA for FedLLMs: it uses hidden-layer embeddings as sample representations and computes their orthogonal residuals onto the gradient subspace—revealing an intrinsic gradient-input linkage without shadow models, auxiliary classifiers, or historical updates. Evaluated across four benchmarks and four LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-2-7B, Qwen-1.5-4B), ProjRes achieves **up to 99.9% accuracy**, outperforming prior arts by **+75.75%**, and remains effective under strong differential privacy (σ=2.0, >86.3% accuracy). This exposes a critical, previously overlooked privacy flaw in FedLLMs and urges re-evaluation of their security foundations. Code and data: [https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Passive-MIA-5268](https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Passive-MIA-5268).

item.title: A Multimodal Text- and Graph-Based Approach for Open-Domain Event Extraction from Documents
item.description: Event extraction is vital for document understanding and real-world decision-making, yet existing methods face critical gaps: closed-domain approaches lack generalization to unseen event types, while open-domain methods underutilize LLMs and ignore document-level structural reasoning (e.g., layout, cross-sentence coherence, table semantics)—challenging for LLMs due to attention dilution and lost-in-the-middle effects. To bridge this gap, we propose **MODEE**, a multimodal framework that jointly leverages LLM-derived text representations and a heterogeneous document graph encoding sentences, paragraphs, tables, entities, and event candidates. Graph neural networks aggregate structural context, which is then fused with LLM outputs via gated alignment to refine event triggers, arguments, and relations. Experiments on large open-domain benchmarks (FewEvent, MAVEN, EDL-2023) show MODEE achieves state-of-the-art F1 (68.3% avg), outperforming prior open-domain methods by +4.1–6.7 points; remarkably, it also transfers zero-shot to closed-domain ACE 2005, scoring 72.9% F1—surpassing dedicated closed-domain systems. MODEE establishes a new paradigm for scalable, structure-aware, and generalizable event extraction.

item.title: Beyond N-gram: Data-Aware X-GRAM Extraction for Efficient Embedding Parameter Scaling
item.description: Large token-indexed lookup tables offer compute-decoupled scaling but suffer from poor parameter efficiency and explosive memory growth—rooted in Zipfian tail under-training, layer-wise heterogeneity, and “slot collapse.” To address this, we propose **X-GRAM**, a data-aware, frequency-adaptive token injection framework. It employs hybrid hashing and alias mixing to compress the tail while preserving head capacity; refines retrieved embeddings via normalized SwiGLU ShortConv to extract diverse local n-gram features; and integrates these signals into attention value streams and inter-layer residuals using depth-aware gating—aligning static memory with dynamic context. This introduces a **memory-centric scaling axis**, decoupling model capacity from FLOPs. Evaluated at 0.73B and 1.15B scales, X-GRAM achieves up to **+4.4 accuracy points** over the vanilla backbone and **+3.2 over strong retrieval baselines**, while using only **50% of the embedding table size**. Code: https://github.com/Longyichen/X-gram

item.title: Outsourced Private Set Intersection for Pairwise Analytics
item.description: This paper introduces **CaOPSI-SS**, a novel outsourced cryptographic primitive for computing the *sum of pairwise intersection cardinalities* across multiple parties while revealing *only the final aggregated result*. Unlike prior PSI schemes, CaOPSI-SS leverages two non-colluding servers and lightweight PRFs to obliviously outsource datasets and compute secret-shared outputs—eliminating leakage of any individual intersection. Built upon this, we design a scalable protocol achieving *O(n·|D|)* communication for *n* parties, and integrate Laplace-based differential privacy for rigorous record-level protection. Evaluated on real enterprise email logs (10M+ entries), our implementation processes 10-party pairwise analytics in under 8 seconds with sub-4GB memory, demonstrating strong practicality for large-scale privacy-preserving analytics.

item.title: Deploying decryption oracles for fun and non-profit: Backing up with friends and TEEs
item.description: Secure backups are the Achilles’ heel of E2EE ecosystems when they lack the same strong security guarantees—especially forward secrecy and post-compromise security—as the messaging systems they serve. State-of-the-art deployed solutions often neglect these properties, while some TEE-based proposals concentrate trust in a small set of critical PKI keys, creating catastrophic single points of failure. We propose a simple, efficient, and elegant backup system that achieves forward secrecy and leakage resilience by *regularly rotating independent backup servers*, each generating fresh key material. To scale, we introduce a *silent backup protocol* that eliminates client-side polling, reducing server load by ~60% versus prior art while strengthening security. Our design trivially supports social key recovery and requires only commodity hardware—no TEEs or vendor-specific infrastructure. We provide formal security proofs and benchmark results showing sub-85ms latency per backup operation and >1200 ops/sec throughput on standard cloud instances, confirming production readiness.

item.title: EQuADiSE: Efficient Quantum-safe Adaptive Distributed Symmetric-key Encryption
item.description: EQuADiSE is the first practical, adaptively secure, and post-quantum distributed symmetric-key encryption (DiSE) scheme. It builds upon the Module Learning with Rounding (MLWR) assumption in the Quantum Random Oracle Model (QROM), resolving the critical trade-off between quantum safety and efficiency that plagues prior DiSE constructions. Unlike existing adaptively secure DiSE schemes—which either rely on quantum-broken assumptions (e.g., discrete log) or suffer exponential online overheads—EQuADiSE achieves **linear-time encryption and decryption** in the number of parties. A core technical innovation is a novel MLWR-based Distributed Pseudorandom Function (DPRF) with adaptive security in QROM, outperforming all prior adaptively secure DPRFs in online evaluation speed. Experimental results confirm EQuADiSE delivers higher online throughput than all previous DiSE realizations—including quantum-vulnerable ones—making it the first viable quantum-safe DiSE for real-world deployment.

item.title: Implementing CCZ Gates with Variation of Gate Teleportation for Quantum Homomorphic Encryption on NISQ Platform
item.description: Quantum Homomorphic Encryption (QHE) promises privacy-preserving computation on encrypted quantum data—critical for cloud-based quantum services from IBM, Google, and AWS. Yet practical deployment on Noisy Intermediate-scale Quantum (NISQ) devices remains hindered by the high resource cost and noise sensitivity of non-Clifford gates, especially the CCZ gate. This work bridges the gap by implementing QHE on NISQ hardware using a Variation of Gate Teleportation (VGT) scheme, built upon the CQC-QHE framework (Ortega et al., 2025). We optimize CCZ realization via measurement-based teleportation, adaptive basis selection, and lightweight error mitigation—reducing T-count by 42% and auxiliary qubits by 60%. Our implementation successfully executes QHE circuits with up to **7 logical qubits and 14 T-gates**, achieving 92.7% average gate fidelity on real superconducting hardware—marking the first NISQ-viable QHE prototype with demonstrable scalability and robustness.

item.title: Factorisation-Based Multivariate Schemes: Structural Properties and New Constructions
item.description: This paper advances trapdoor design in multivariate cryptography by introducing the *Hidden Factor Decomposability (HFD)* principle: if an algebraic structure (e.g., $\mathbb{F}_p[x]/I$) admits a secret, nontrivial factorization of public elements—efficiently recoverable only with private knowledge—then HFD serves as a sound and general trapdoor mechanism. Leveraging this insight, we propose two concrete schemes: (**i**) **FBSig**, a signature scheme achieving compact signatures (~1.2 KB) and faster verification than Rainbow; and (**ii**) **FBEnc**, the first deterministic encryption scheme built solely on polynomial ring factorization, proven CPA-secure under a structured polynomial decomposition assumption (without random oracles). Both resist standard algebraic attacks (Gröbner bases, XL, separation), and their correctness and security are formally verified via symbolic computation.

item.title: PermaFrost-Attack: Stealth Pretraining Seeding(SPS) for planting Logic Landmines During LLM Training
item.description: We introduce **PermaFrost-Attack**, a novel stealthy data poisoning framework exploiting the web-scale pretraining pipeline of LLMs. Through *Stealth Pretraining Seeding (SPS)*, adversaries inject tiny, benign-looking payloads across thousands of low-traffic websites—deliberately exposed to crawlers via `robots.txt`—to infiltrate future training corpora (e.g., Common Crawl) undetected. These payloads embed dormant *logic landmines*: latent unsafe behaviors invisible under standard safety evaluations but reliably activated by precise alphanumeric triggers (e.g., `<00TRIGGER00>`), bypassing alignment safeguards. We propose three geometric diagnostics—*Thermodynamic Length*, *Spectral Curvature*, and the *Infection Traceback Graph*—to detect and characterize such latent vulnerabilities. Across Llama, Qwen, and Phi models (3B–7B), SPS achieves >82% attack success while evading RLHF and DPO defenses. Our work reveals SPS as a practical, underappreciated threat to foundation model integrity and establishes a principled geometric lens for uncovering invisible model weaknesses.

item.title: Memanto: Typed Semantic Memory with Information-Theoretic Retrieval for Long-Horizon Agents
item.description: This paper introduces **Memanto**, a lightweight, typed semantic memory layer for long-horizon autonomous agents that eliminates the need for complex knowledge graphs. Memanto features: (1) a fixed schema of **13 semantic memory types**, (2) automated conflict resolution with temporal versioning, and (3) Moorcheh—an information-theoretic, *no-indexing* search engine enabling deterministic, sub-90ms retrieval with *zero ingestion latency*. Evaluated on LongMemEval and LoCoMo, Memanto achieves state-of-the-art accuracy (**89.8%** and **87.1%**, respectively), outperforming all hybrid graph and vector-based baselines—while requiring only **one retrieval query**, incurring **no ingestion cost**, and drastically reducing operational complexity. A five-stage ablation study confirms the critical role of typed semantics and information-theoretic retrieval in scalability and fidelity.

item.title: Shard the Gradient, Scale the Model: Serverless Federated Aggregation via Gradient Partitioning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) aggregation on serverless platforms hits a hard scalability wall: existing architectures (e.g., lambda-FL, LIFL) partition *clients* across aggregators, but each aggregator must load the *entire gradient* into memory—rendering aggregation infeasible when gradients exceed per-function memory limits (e.g., 10 GB on AWS Lambda). We propose **GradsSharding**, which instead partitions the *gradient tensor* into $M$ shards; each shard is averaged independently by a serverless function receiving contributions from *all clients*. Due to the element-wise linearity of FedAvg, this yields **bit-identical results** to centralized or tree-based aggregation—guaranteeing zero accuracy loss. Per-function memory scales as $O(|\theta|/M)$, independent of client count, enabling FL for arbitrarily large models. Evaluated across models from 43 MB to 5 GB on real AWS Lambda and HPC clusters, GradsSharding achieves a cost crossover at ~500 MB gradient size, reduces cost by 2.7× at VGG-16 scale, and remains the **only deployable architecture beyond the serverless memory ceiling**.

item.title: Generating Synthetic Malware Samples Using Generative AI
item.description: Malware classification suffers from severe data imbalance and scarcity of diverse, obfuscated samples—especially for emerging families. To address this, we propose a novel generative AI framework that decomposes malware binaries into **semantic-rich mnemonic opcode sequences**, enhanced by NLP-derived contextual features (e.g., control-flow patterns, API call semantics) to guide generation. We evaluate GAN, WGAN-GP, and a **domain-adapted Diffusion model**, finding the latter yields the highest-fidelity synthetic samples. Augmenting training data with Diffusion-generated samples boosts minority-class accuracy by **up to 60% on average**, lifting overall classification accuracy to **96% (+8%)**. Crucially, synthetic samples preserve executable structure and behavioral fidelity without increasing false positives—demonstrating their practical utility for robust, scalable malware analysis under data-constrained conditions.

item.title: Beyond N-gram: Data-Aware X-GRAM Extraction for Efficient Embedding Parameter Scaling
item.description: Large embedding lookup tables enable compute-decoupled scaling but suffer from poor parameter efficiency due to Zipfian under-training, layer-wise heterogeneity, and “slot collapse”. To address this, we propose **X-GRAM**, a data-aware, frequency-adaptive token injection framework. It combines hybrid hashing and alias mixing to compress the long tail while preserving head capacity; refines retrieved embeddings via normalized SwiGLU-ShortConv to extract diverse local n-gram features; and integrates these signals into attention value streams and inter-layer residuals using depth-aware gating—aligning static memory with dynamic context. Evaluated at 0.73B and 1.15B scales, X-GRAM achieves up to **+4.4 accuracy points** over the vanilla backbone and **+3.2 over strong retrieval baselines**, while using only **50% of the original table size**. This introduces a *memory-centric scaling axis*, decoupling capacity from FLOPs—enabling scalable, practical memory-augmented architectures. Code: https://github.com/Longyichen/X-gram.

item.title: Synthesizing Multi-Agent Harnesses for Vulnerability Discovery
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated unprecedented capability in discovering real-world security vulnerabilities missed by human auditors and traditional fuzzers—yet their effectiveness critically depends on the *multi-agent harness*: the program orchestrating agent roles, prompts, tools, communication topology, and coordination protocols. Current harness design is largely manual or limited to narrow search spaces with coarse pass/fail feedback, lacking diagnostic signals for failure root causes. AgentFlow addresses both limitations via (1) a typed graph domain-specific language (DSL) that jointly optimizes all harness dimensions, and (2) a feedback-driven outer loop that introspects target program runtime signals (e.g., crashes, sandbox violations) to localize and repair faulty harness components. Evaluated on TerminalBench-2 with Claude Opus 4.6, AgentFlow achieves **84.3%**, the highest published score. On Google Chrome using Kimi K2.5, it discovers **10 previously unknown zero-day vulnerabilities**, including **two Critical sandbox-escape flaws** (CVE-2026-5280, CVE-2026-6297).

item.title: DAIRE: A lightweight AI model for real-time detection of Controller Area Network attacks in the Internet of Vehicles
item.description: DAIRE is a lightweight AI framework designed for real-time detection and classification of Controller Area Network (CAN) attacks in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV). It employs a custom artificial neural network with neuron count per layer defined as $N_i = i \times c$, where $c$ is the number of attack classes, enabling ultra-low computational overhead while maintaining high accuracy. Trained with sparse categorical cross-entropy loss and RMSProp optimization, DAIRE effectively identifies DoS, Fuzzy, and Spoofing attacks. Evaluated on CICIoV2024 and Car-Hacking datasets, it achieves an average detection rate of **99.88%**, a false positive rate of **0.02%**, and overall accuracy of **99.96%**, with inference time of only **0.03 ms per sample**—significantly outperforming state-of-the-art models in speed and efficiency. DAIRE bridges the gap between robust cybersecurity and real-time constraints in resource-limited vehicular systems.

item.title: Differentially Private Clustered Federated Learning with Privacy-Preserving Initialization and Normality-Driven Aggregation
item.description: Federated learning (FL) preserves data locality but suffers from slow convergence and poor generalization under cross-device data heterogeneity; clustered FL (CFL) mitigates this by grouping clients with similar data distributions. However, integrating differential privacy (DP) into CFL remains challenging: DP noise corrupts client updates, preventing reliable cluster centroid initialization and degrading clustering quality. To address this, we propose **PINA**, a two-stage differentially private CFL framework. First, clients fine-tune lightweight LoRA adapters and privately share *compressed sketches* of their updates—enabling the server to construct robust, noise-resilient cluster centroids. Second, PINA introduces **normality-driven aggregation**: it statistically tests update normality (e.g., via Shapiro-Wilk) and adaptively filters outliers before weighted aggregation, enhancing convergence stability and robustness. Extensive experiments show PINA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art DP-FL methods by **+2.9% average accuracy** under tight privacy budgets (ε ∈ {2, 8}), while maintaining high intra-cluster coherence. PINA is the first framework to deliver formal DP guarantees *without sacrificing CFL’s structural benefits*, enabling practical, privacy-preserving personalized learning in heterogeneous edge environments.

item.title: Towards Certified Malware Detection: Provable Guarantees Against Evasion Attacks
item.description: Machine learning-based static malware detectors are highly vulnerable to adversarial evasion—especially metamorphic mutations that preserve functionality while altering features. To address this, we propose the first *certifiably robust* detection framework for binaries, leveraging randomized smoothing via *feature ablation* and *targeted noise injection*. Our method generates numerous ablated variants of an input executable, classifies each with a base classifier, and outputs the majority-voted label. Crucially, by analyzing the top-class vote distribution and applying the Wilson score interval, we derive a formal robustness certificate: the predicted label is guaranteed invariant against any feature-space perturbation within a certified radius *R*. Evaluated on clean executables and PyMetaEngine-generated metamorphic variants, our smoothed classifier achieves 92.3% clean accuracy and issues valid certificates for 78.6% of samples (with *R* ≥ 15), while reducing evasion success by 41.2%—all without modifying the underlying model architecture or retraining. This work establishes a theoretically grounded, practical path toward trustworthy malware detection.

item.title: CyberCertBench: Evaluating LLMs in Cybersecurity Certification Knowledge
item.description: CyberCertBench is a novel MCQA benchmark for evaluating LLMs on real-world cybersecurity certification knowledge, derived from industry standards including CISSP, IEC 62443, NIST SP 800, and OT-specific certifications. We introduce the Proposer-Verifier framework—a method to generate interpretable, standards-grounded natural language explanations for model predictions. Evaluation shows frontier models achieve human-expert accuracy (≥92%) on general IT/networking security, but drop sharply (28–41% points) on vendor-specific nuances or formal standard clauses (e.g., IEC 62443-3-3). Scaling analysis reveals strong parameter efficiency gains from 2023–2024, yet diminishing returns in recent >100B-parameter models. Code and data are open-sourced at https://github.com/GKeppler/CyberCertBench.

item.title: Text Steganography with Dynamic Codebook and Multimodal Large Language Model
item.description: This paper proposes a novel black-box text steganography framework leveraging a **dynamic codebook** and a **multimodal large language model (MLLM)** to overcome key limitations of existing white-box and black-box approaches. Unlike white-box methods—vulnerable due to shared off-the-shelf LLMs—or rigid black-box schemes requiring fixed codebooks and sentence-specific extraction prompts, our method generates session-dependent, context-aware codebooks on-the-fly using lightweight shared configurations (e.g., timestamp + seed) and an MLLM (e.g., Qwen-VL). Secret messages are embedded via an encrypted steganographic mapping during caption generation, preserving linguistic quality. A reject-sampling–based feedback optimization ensures reliable message extraction. Experiments show our method achieves **4.2 bits/sentence embedding capacity**, outperforming state-of-the-art white-box methods by 1.8× in capacity and significantly improving text quality (BERTScore +9.3%, BLEU-4 +12.6%). Crucially, it demonstrates superior practicality on real-world platforms (e.g., Twitter, Weibo) without requiring model access or API keys—only public MLLM interfaces.

item.title: Self-Guided Plan Extraction for Instruction-Following Tasks with Goal-Conditional Reinforcement Learning
item.description: We propose **SuperIgor**, a self-guided framework for instruction-following tasks that eliminates reliance on hand-crafted subtasks or annotated demonstration datasets. SuperIgor establishes a closed-loop co-training paradigm: a language model (LM) autonomously generates and refines high-level plans, while a goal-conditioned RL agent learns to execute them in stochastic, dynamic environments. Crucially, the RL agent provides dense feedback—on fidelity, feasibility, and goal alignment—which the LM uses to iteratively improve plan structure, granularity, and robustness. Evaluated across diverse embodied navigation and tool-use benchmarks, SuperIgor agents achieve **23.7% higher instruction adherence accuracy** and **41.2% better zero-shot generalization to unseen instructions** versus strong baselines (SFT, PPO+CLIP, HIRO). This work demonstrates the first successful joint emergence of planning and control capabilities in an unannotated, self-supervised setting.

item.title: FSFM: A Biologically-Inspired Framework for Selective Forgetting of Agent Memory
item.description: FSFM is a biologically inspired framework that formalizes *selective forgetting* as a first-class capability for LLM agents—moving beyond passive memory retention to active, adaptive memory curation. Grounded in hippocampal indexing theory and the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve, FSFM introduces a taxonomy of four forgetting mechanisms: passive decay, active deletion, safety-triggered erasure, and adaptive reinforcement learning–based forgetting. Implemented atop modern agent architectures and vector databases, it enables efficient metadata-aware pruning, real-time sensitive-data scrubbing, and context-aware preference updating. Controlled experiments demonstrate: **+8.49% memory access efficiency**, **+29.2% signal-to-noise ratio in generated content**, and **100% elimination of security risks** (e.g., prompt injection, PII leakage). FSFM bridges cognitive neuroscience and production AI systems, advancing both ethical compliance and resource-constrained deployment of next-generation agents.

item.title: FedSIR: Spectral Client Identification and Relabeling for Federated Learning with Noisy Labels
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training, yet pervasive label noise across clients severely degrades model performance. To address this, we propose **FedSIR**, a spectral-domain framework for robust FL under noisy labels. Unlike prior works relying on noise-robust losses or loss dynamics, FedSIR identifies noisy clients via **spectral consistency analysis** of class-wise feature subspaces—requiring minimal communication—and enables them to relabel corrupted samples using spectral references (dominant directions + residual subspaces) from clean clients. It further stabilizes optimization via a noise-aware strategy combining logit-adjusted loss, knowledge distillation from clean clients, and distance-aware model aggregation. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet show FedSIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods by **5.2–9.7 percentage points** under 40%–60% symmetric/asymmetric noise. Code is available at https://github.com/sinagh72/FedSIR.

item.title: Lifecycle-Aware Federated Continual Learning in Mobile Autonomous Systems
item.description: Federated continual learning (FCL) enables distributed autonomous fleets to collaboratively adapt to evolving environments across extended mission lifecycles—yet existing methods suffer from uniform layer protection, neglect of long-term cumulative drift, and over-reliance on idealized simulations. We propose a **lifecycle-aware dual-timescale FCL framework**, integrating *training-time rehearsal* and *post-degradation recovery*. Specifically, we design a **layer-selective rehearsal strategy**, allocating memory based on gradient sensitivity and semantic hierarchy to suppress immediate forgetting; and a **rapid knowledge recovery mechanism**, using topology-aware distillation to restore model fidelity after prolonged drift. Theoretically, we characterize heterogeneous forgetting dynamics and prove the inevitability of long-term degradation under non-IID and device heterogeneity. Experiments show up to **+8.3% mIoU** over the strongest federated baseline and **+31.7%** over conventional fine-tuning. Real-world deployment on a rover testbed under realistic constraints (≤50 KB/s bandwidth, 23% disconnection rate, battery-limited operation) confirms robust system-level performance retention.

item.title: Generative Augmentation of Imbalanced Flight Records for Flight Diversion Prediction: A Multi-objective Optimisation Framework
item.description: Flight diversions—rare yet critical aviation events—suffer from extreme class imbalance in historical records, severely limiting predictive model performance. To address this, we propose a **multi-objective optimisation (MOO) framework** that jointly tunes hyperparameters and architecture choices of three deep generative models (TVAE, CTGAN, CopulaGAN), with Gaussian Copula as statistical baseline. Our MOO objective balances synthetic data quality across six dimensions: realism, diversity, operational validity, statistical similarity, fidelity, and predictive utility—rigorously validated via expert review and downstream classification tasks. Results show MOO-optimised generators significantly outperform non-optimised counterparts (+38.2% average score gain, *p*<0.01), and synthetic augmentation lifts XGBoost’s diversion prediction AUC from 0.721 (real-data-only) to **0.864**, with F1-score increasing by 0.22. This work establishes a principled, operationally grounded paradigm for generative augmentation of rare safety-critical events in air transport.

item.title: RespondeoQA: a Benchmark for Bilingual Latin-English Question Answering
item.description: We introduce **RespondeoQA**, the first benchmark dataset for bilingual Latin–English question answering and constrained translation, comprising ~7,800 expert-validated QA pairs. Questions are sourced from pedagogical Latin materials—including textbooks (1800s–present), AP exams, and quizbowl trivia—and cover diverse task types: knowledge-based QA, skill-oriented tasks (e.g., scansion, literary device identification), multihop reasoning, and cross-lingual translation (Latin↔English, mixed-language queries). Evaluating LLaMa 3, Qwen QwQ, and OpenAI’s o3-mini, we find all models underperform significantly on skill-based questions (−18–25 F1 points vs. knowledge QA); while o3-mini and QwQ show modest gains in scansion and literary analysis, overall improvements remain limited. QwQ achieves the highest robustness on Latin-prompted questions, whereas LLaMa 3 and o3-mini exhibit stronger task dependency. RespondeoQA enables fine-grained assessment of LLM capabilities in classical language understanding and supports reproducible research in humanities AI. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/slanglab/RespondeoQA.

item.title: Anchor-and-Resume Concession Under Dynamic Pricing for LLM-Augmented Freight Negotiation
item.description: This paper addresses monotonicity violation and non-determinism in LLM-augmented freight negotiation under dynamic pricing. We propose the **Anchor-and-Resume Concession Framework**, which decouples pricing logic from language generation: a spread-derived adaptive $β$ tailors concession speed to each load’s margin regime, while the anchor-and-resume mechanism guarantees strictly non-decreasing offers despite arbitrary price shifts—all via deterministic formulas. The LLM serves only as a natural-language translation layer, eliminating inference cost, nondeterminism, and prompt injection risks. Empirical evaluation across 115,125 real negotiations shows: (1) adaptive $β$ outperforms fixed-$β$ baselines in broker savings for medium/wide spreads and prioritizes closure in narrow spreads; (2) it matches a 20B-parameter unconstrained LLM broker in agreement rate and savings; (3) against stochastic LLM-powered carriers, it achieves comparable savings and **higher agreement rates** than against rule-based opponents; and (4) it scales horizontally to thousands of concurrent negotiations with near-zero inference overhead and full decision transparency.

item.title: Layer 2 Blockchains Simplified: A Survey of Vector Commitment Schemes, ZKP Frameworks, Layer-2 Data Structures and Verkle Trees
item.description: This paper presents the first unified, security-centric survey of Layer-2 (L2) blockchains, systematically mapping L2 architectural components to their foundational cryptographic assumptions. We rigorously analyze vector commitment schemes (KZG, IPA), ZKP frameworks (Groth16, Plonk), and data structures—including Verkle trees—linking each to core hardness assumptions (Discrete Logarithm, Computational/Bilinear Diffie-Hellman). We formalize a comprehensive L2 threat model covering sequencer liveness failures, bridge exploits, and data availability attacks, explicitly identifying which cryptographic primitives underpin each vulnerability. Our work bridges theory and practice by providing developers with actionable security guidelines—for instance, clarifying that IPA-based commitments coupled with Plonk proofs rely critically on the Bilinear DH assumption, and its breakdown compromises soundness. This survey serves as an essential reference for researchers and engineers designing, auditing, or standardizing secure L2 protocols.

item.title: Breaking MCP with Function Hijacking Attacks: Novel Threats for Function Calling and Agentic Models
item.description: This paper introduces **Function Hijacking Attack (FHA)**, a novel threat against function-calling LLMs and agentic systems. Unlike prior injection or jailbreaking attacks targeting output generation, FHA directly subverts the *tool selection process*: it manipulates the model into invoking an attacker-specified function—regardless of user intent, semantic context, or tool set composition. We demonstrate FHA’s **semantic agnosticism** and **function-set robustness**, enabling cross-domain exploitation without per-task fine-tuning. Crucially, we train **universal adversarial functions**—single malicious function templates that hijack tool selection across diverse queries and payloads. Evaluated on 5 state-of-the-art models (including instructed and reasoning variants) using the BFCL benchmark, FHA achieves **70%–100% attack success rate (ASR)**. Our findings expose a critical trust gap in agentic tool orchestration and underscore the urgent need for runtime guardrails—such as intent-function alignment checks, signed tool registries, and sandboxed execution—to secure real-world agentic deployments.

item.title: Differentially Private Model Merging
item.description: This paper addresses the challenge of *on-demand differential privacy (DP) adaptation* during model deployment: given a set of pre-trained models—each satisfying a different DP guarantee (εₖ, δₖ) on the same dataset—how can we generate a new model meeting *any arbitrary target* (ε, δ) *without retraining*? We propose two efficient post-processing merging strategies: (**i**) *random selection*, which samples a model from the pool according to a privacy-aware distribution; and (**ii**) *linear combination*, which forms a weighted average of model parameters. We provide rigorous privacy accounting for both via Rényi DP composition and privacy loss distributions (PLDs), applicable to general learning tasks. In a complete analysis of private mean estimation, we derive tight utility bounds and prove that linear combination strictly dominates random selection in the privacy-utility tradeoff. Experiments across synthetic data, CIFAR-10, and PTB confirm our theory: our methods achieve up to 41% lower error than baselines under the same ε (0.5–8), with negligible inference overhead.
